lens.included_activities: LMM operating firms supplying recurring outsourced portfolio-management or investment-advice services to external investor clients.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive family offices and internal investment units, shells, control-financing vehicles, execution-only broker activity, and firms without a recurring or repeat external service relationship.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External individual or institutional clients receiving recurring advice, monitoring, discretionary management, or planning; revenue is commonly an advisory fee, AUM-based fee, retainer, flat fee, commission, or performance-based fee where permitted.

a.bridge: S2 measures US personal financial advisors, not wage-weighted employment in NAICS 523940. I map its listed recurring tasks of research, account monitoring, explaining options, preparing advice, and answering routine questions to AI-exposed work, while retaining relationship, judgment, sales, and accountable recommendation work as less substitutable. This proxy likely understates exposure for portfolio-management firms with more investment-analysis and reporting operations than the source occupation.
a.rationale: A material but minority share of present labor can be reduced through research synthesis, meeting preparation, communications, monitoring, reporting, and workflow automation. Client-specific judgment, relationship building, sales, and fiduciary accountability remain substantial wage-bearing work.
a.change_evidence: A 2026 OEWS occupation-by-industry wage file for 523940, paired with time-and-motion interviews at LMM RIAs and portfolio managers that separate advisers, analysts, compliance, and client-service hours.
caveats: The proxy includes self-employed advisers and advisers working outside the target NAICS.
No source measures current AI exposure or wage shares for the frozen LMM lens.

rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should reach routine research, drafting, service, surveillance, and internal knowledge workflows, but regulated advice requires review, recordkeeping, supervision, model validation, and careful client communication. SEC enforcement over misleading AI claims makes unchecked deployment especially unsuitable.
rho.change_evidence: A representative RIA operations survey showing production use by workflow, documented AI governance policies, and examination or enforcement outcomes involving AI-assisted advice.
caveats: This is an operational-adoption judgment, not a forecast of AI vendor revenue or client acceptance.
Implementation will differ sharply between retail advice, institutional portfolio management, and firms with mature compliance teams.

e.bridge: S1 reports the size distribution of investment advisers, not normalized EBITDA or the frozen NAICS/lens population. I use the reported prevalence of advisers with 100 or fewer employees and the eight-employee average for individual-client advisers as a proxy for the share plausibly inside the LMM operating-firm band after excluding captive and non-service entities. It likely overstates eligibility because a small headcount can still support EBITDA above the band, especially at high AUM.
e.rationale: The adviser market contains many small practices, yet the target EBITDA band excludes very small owner-operator books, high-margin firms with substantial AUM, and entities outside the external recurring-service lens.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Form ADV data joined to private-company financials or confidential QoE reports that identify revenue, compensation, EBITDA, client type, and ownership for 523940 businesses.
caveats: Employee count and AUM are weak substitutes for normalized EBITDA.
S1 is an investment-adviser population, whereas NAICS 523940 also includes portfolio-management businesses with different economics.

s5.bridge: S5 counts 2025 RIA transactions with at least $100 million AUM, while S1 counts advisers and neither isolates LMM EBITDA firms or only qualifying control transfers. I use the recorded transaction activity as evidence of a functioning transfer market and translate its annual incidence into a conservative five-year probability for eligible firms. The proxy likely overstates the LMM rate because S5 says smaller sellers declined in both count and share and excludes sub-$100 million-AUM deals from its methodology.
s5.rationale: RIA transactions are active, but the observed market is concentrated in larger sellers and repeat acquirers. A minority of qualifying LMM firms should transfer control within five years; internal succession, client portability, founder dependence, and buyer selectivity limit the rate.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year longitudinal extract of Form ADV ownership changes and Form ADV-W filings matched to transaction announcements, with deal terms coded for control, firm size, and internal versus external succession.
caveats: AUM thresholds are not EBITDA thresholds.
Transaction announcements can omit private, internal, and failed processes and do not by themselves identify control transfers.

q.rationale: Recurring advisory arrangements can retain a meaningful share of workflow savings because fees are commonly set in advance and are not automatically cost-plus. Retention is capped by transparent fee schedules, negotiability, client expectations, competitive rebids, and the need to reinvest savings in service and compliance.
q.change_evidence: A sample of LMM advisory agreements and Form ADV brochures before and after workflow automation, paired with client retention, fee-rate, and service-level changes at renewal.
caveats: The SEC disclosure source identifies fee schedules and negotiability but does not measure savings pass-through.
AUM-fee, flat-fee, commission, and performance-fee models retain savings differently.

d5.bridge: S2 projects US personal-financial-advisor employment to rise 10 percent from 2024 to 2034, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for the NAICS 523940 service basket. I convert that ten-year occupation-growth proxy to an approximately five-year demand ratio and widen the range for productivity and non-target employment. It likely overstates codewide demand because the occupation includes self-employed and financial-planning work outside the target industry.
d5.rationale: Aging clients, longer retirements, and the shift from traditional pensions to individual retirement accounts support ongoing advice demand. Robo-advisors may temper routine demand, while complex or specialized advice continues to require human service.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year constant-dollar series for 523940 advisory revenue and client accounts, segmented by retail, institutional, discretionary, and non-discretionary services, plus price-index data for advisory fees.
caveats: Employment is not service quantity and could change with productivity or staffing models.
The forecast is national and not limited to LMM firms or outsourced providers.

o.rationale: Most surviving demand should still need an accountable operator for client discovery, suitability and best-interest obligations, nuanced planning, relationship management, and review of recommendations. Software can eliminate some routine allocation and reporting service, especially for simpler accounts.
o.change_evidence: Client-level adoption and attrition data comparing digital-only and human-advised accounts, and supervisory records showing which advice, monitoring, and disclosure duties remain assigned to humans after AI deployment.
caveats: This is a judgment about the operator requirement, not a claim that software cannot provide investment advice.
Institutional clients and mass-affluent retail clients may retain human accountability at different rates.

narrative.executive_view: This is a recurring, relationship-heavy financial service with a large small-adviser population, active transfer market, and constrained but real workflow automation potential. The code combines retail and institutional practices, so LMM diligence must distinguish portable recurring client relationships from founder-dependent books and non-target entities.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can accelerate investment research, meeting preparation, draft communications, account monitoring, documentation, reporting, and internal knowledge retrieval. It is less able to replace accountable, client-specific recommendations, relationship trust, sales, and supervised fiduciary judgment; SEC actions over false AI claims reinforce the need for controlled deployment.
narrative.value_capture: Advisers must disclose compensation, fee schedules, and fee negotiability in Form ADV materials. That supports retention where recurring fees are set contractually, but visible pricing, competitive alternatives, and renewal discussions can return some savings to clients or require reinvestment in service and compliance.
narrative.firm_availability: The IAA reports that 92.8 percent of advisers had 100 or fewer employees in 2025 and that individual-client advisers averaged eight employees, indicating a deep small-practice population. DeVoe recorded active RIA transactions, but its data show buyer attention and deal volume concentrating above smaller sellers, so transferability for the target LMM band is uncertain.
narrative.demand_durability: BLS projects 10 percent growth in personal-financial-advisor employment from 2024 to 2034, citing aging clients, longer retirements, and individual retirement saving. It also expects robo-advisors to temper routine demand while complex and specialized advice continues to use human advisers, supporting a modest-growth demand view rather than a software-elimination view.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: No public source directly measures 523940 LMM EBITDA, AI-exposed wage-weighted tasks, implementation, qualifying control transfers, savings pass-through, or constant-quality service quantity. Regulatory supervision, client portability, market-sensitive AUM fees, heterogeneous business models, and an M&A market favoring larger sellers can all materially change the outcome.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring advice relationships and substantial research, service, monitoring, and documentation workflows create a credible but bounded operating-improvement opportunity.
narrative.principal_weakness: Public evidence does not isolate the LMM transferable-firm subset, and fiduciary accountability plus founder/client dependence limit both automation and transferability.

sources.title: Investment Adviser Association, Industry Statistics
sources.supports: Reports 16,544 advisers in 2025, 92.8 percent with 100 or fewer employees, 67.4 percent with less than $1 billion AUM, and an eight-employee average for advisers focused on individuals.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Personal Financial Advisors
sources.supports: Lists advisor duties, 326,000 2024 jobs, 10 percent projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034, and BLS's explanation that aging, retirement duration, and individual retirement saving raise demand while robo-advisors temper it.

sources.title: Investor.gov: Form ADV Investment Adviser Brochure and Brochure Supplement
sources.supports: States that advisers disclose advisory business, fees and compensation, fee schedule, negotiability, billing, conflicts, methods of analysis, strategies, and risks in Form ADV brochure materials.

sources.title: SEC Charges Two Investment Advisers with False and Misleading AI Statements
sources.supports: Reports March 2024 settled charges against two investment advisers for false and misleading AI-use statements and total civil penalties of 400,000 dollars.

sources.title: DeVoe & Company, Fourth Quarter 2025 RIA Deal Book
sources.supports: Reports 322 tracked RIA transactions in 2025, 165 consolidator transactions, 90 RIA-buyer transactions, 67 other-buyer transactions, and that smaller sellers declined in count and share; the analysis covers sellers with at least 100 million dollars AUM.
